Einstein said to Chaplin: “What I most admire about your art, is your universality. You don’t say a word, yet the world understands you!”.  

"It’s true”, replied Chaplin. “But your fame is even greater: the world admires you, when nobody understands what you say."  

 - Albert Einstein and Charlie Chaplin, 1931

Statistically speaking, there are likely hundreds of people walking among us today whose raw intelligence is equal to Albert Einstein's—perhaps even greater. Yet few of them, if any, are likely to be immortalized years from now as a prototypical "genius," as Ein

Albert Einstein did not work on the Manhattan Project and did not help design or build the atomic weapons used in WW2 but he played an indirect role in its development.

In 1939 Einstein signed a letter to President Franklin D Roosevelt that had been drafted primarily by physicist Leo Szilard. The letter warned that Germany might be attempting to develop extremely powerful new weapons based on uranium and urged the United States government to take the research seriously. Einstein’s international reputation gave the warning significant political weight. That letter helped encourage the US government to begin funding research that eventually became the Manhattan Project.

Einstein himself was a theoretical physicist best known for the equation E equals mc squared, which demonstrated that mass can be converted into vast amounts of energy. While this principle later proved fundamental to nuclear physics, Einstein did not work on nuclear chain reactions or weapons engineering. He was not gi
